title: The Macro Minute: Did “TACO Trump” just permanently cede control of global energy markets to Iran?

Happy Monday out there, team 42, it's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our macro minute for Monday,
March 23rd, 2026. Hope everyone had a
great weekend. So, as always, we start
with the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
did Taco Trump just permanently cede
control of global energy markets to
Iran?
Our answer is maybe. We'll find out in
{quote} 5 days.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis. We'll start
with the positioning cycle because I
think that's the most important thing to
call out today.
Um so, a a prospective easing of Middle
East tensions catalyzed a cross-asset
relief rally after President Trump
signaled a delay in strikes on Iranian
energy infrastructure citing
constructive diplomacy towards conflict
resolution.
Specifically, POTUS deferred previously
threatened military action targeting
Iranian energy and power assets by 5
days contingent on negotiations aimed at
ending the conflict.
President Trump disclosed that both
parties engaged in {quote} very good and
productive conversations regarding a
complete and total resolution of our
hostilities in the Middle East via Truth
Social post at 7:23 a.m. Eastern today.
Iranian officials, via the semi-official
Fars News Agency, denied any direct or
indirect communication with Trump.
Markets were whipsawed by the sudden
pivot as traders had observed no prior
evidence of diplomatic traction,
particularly with Israel initiating
strikes on Iranian infrastructure hours
earlier, and Tehran retaliating across
Gulf states.
Uh shifting to uh the growth cycle,
nothing actionable with respect to our
Bayesian priors. Regarding the inflation
cycle, which I would argue is equally as
important as the positioning cycle
dynamic this morning. Recall that
President Trump imposed a Monday evening
deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait
of Hormuz or face coordinated US and
Israeli strikes on power infrastructure
prompting Iran to threaten retaliatory
retaliatory tax on regional energy,
information technology, and water
systems. Also, recall that Iran's
National Defense Council warned Monday
that it would mine the entire Persian
Gulf if further attacks targeted its
coastline.
From our qualified vantage point, and
I'm someone who learned geopolitical
game theory at Yale from arguably the
grea- the the greatest geopolitical game
theorists in history, uh John Gaddis, uh
took a few of his classes at Yale, uh it
appears that Iran will exit this
conflict with even more control of the
Strait of Hormuz, and as such, will
dictate the price of global energy
products as the purveyor of marginal
supply. Let me read this again.
From our qualified vantage point, it
appears that Iran will exit this
conflict with even more control of the
Strait of Hormuz, and as such, will
dictate the price of global energy
products as the purveyor of marginal
supply.
Given that this is a very untenable
outcome for even Taco Trump, investors
should be wary of any pullback in trader
tradeable energy goods prices over the
next few days. Said differently, if I
were running Iran, leaving diplomatic
negotiations with anything less than
this outcome would be a failure of
common sense.
You've already seen the taco come out if
you're Iran.
So, now is the time to press the
advantages, not the time to give this
man an easy out from the conflict that
he started.
So, in my opinion, uh uh
I wouldn't be I would be very wary as an
investor of any relief rally right now
because it could be if I'm Iran, based
on everything I learned at Yale from the
great John Gaddis, now is the time to
step on the enemy's throat and make sure
you walk away with control of the Strait
of Hormuz permanently. And so, we'll
see.
Uh
getting to monetary policy, uh Federal
Reserve Bank of Chicago President Austan
Goolsbee indicated a bimodal
distribution of monetary policy tied to
geopolitical outcomes, noting that the
Fed could either tighten or ease
depending on how the Middle East
conflict resolves. Goolsbee stated on a
Monday uh CNBC interview that {quote} we
could be back to the environment with
multiple rate cuts for the year if
inflation behaves. And he added, I could
see circumstances where we would need to
raise rates if it was going different uh
going a different way and inflation was
getting out of control, which is
something we warned about last week,
recall in our uh macro minute from last
uh Thursday. Uh Goolsbee, a non-voting
member uh this year, assessed that the
Fed is {quote} closer to full employment
than we are on the target uh on the
inflation side uh at the moment. I think
so I think inflation has got to be a
little bit ahead of the employment in
the policy prioritization. Elsewhere,
Fed Governor Christopher Waller
acknowledged on Bloomberg uh television
earlier today that sustained elevated
oil prices could transmit into broader
goods and services inflation, but
dismissed the need to consider further
rate hikes at this state consider rate
hikes at this stage. Uh Waller stated uh
we should {quote} wait for all the
information to come in before really
changing our outlook, and added, I think
it's still premature to have a clear
view about uh what this is going to look
like uh as you look 12 months out.
Recall that Fed Chair Jerome Powell
reiterated uh post-decision last week
that his rate at rate hikes are not just
are not the base case for {quote} vast
majority of officials, while also
stressing uncertainty around the
magnitude and duration of the Middle
East conflict and its implications for
uh inflation and growth. Uh you know,
this is what we're talking about um you
know, last week, which is the
distribution of probable monetary policy
outcomes, not just for the Fed, but in
terms of the signals we got from the
ECB, the Bank of Japan, and the Bank of
England last week, distribution has
evolved. It was negatively skewed uh in
in all those cases, and now it's a lot
more uh uh Gaussian, I would argue
bimodal. You know, there's a chance that
they're hiking rates and tightening
monetary policy uh
uh um you know, at some point over the
medium term, or uh going back to uh
doing nothing and/or cutting. And so,
now we have this bimodal distribution,
and obviously we've seen the impact of
the the evolution from a negatively
skewed distribution with respect to
policy rates to a bimodal distribution
with respect to policy rates in terms of
the impact we've seen on global fixed
income uh markets uh and really just
across the volatility complex with uh
bond volatility, currency volatility,
equity volatility, credit spread all
breaking out to bullish from the
perspective of our volatility just
momentum signal uh in recent weeks. Uh
with respect to fiscal policy, nothing
actionable with respect to our Bayesian
priors. And then lastly, with the with
respect to the liquidity cycle, uh in
China, the PBOC's decision to leave its
benchmark policy rates unchanged is
inconsistent with our outlook for
slowing below-trend inflation. PBOC has
largely refrained from lowering its
policy rates since early 2025, and
instead has accelerated direct liquidity
support for the Chinese banking sector,
likely to curtail the impact of
persistent capital outflows upon the
renminbi. As a result of the PBOC's
careful easing strategy, which itself
stems from the impossible trinity of
monetary policy, liquidity in China is
expanding 27% on a 3-month annualized
basis through February, outpacing all
other major liquidity-supplying
economies. Uh Eurozone liquidity is up
11% on a 3-month annualized basis,
Japan's down 2%, Switzerland's up 15%,
the UK is up 5%, and the US is up 4%.
These positive deltas through February,
these are monthly data points through
February, uh have reversed sharply on a
monthly day basis because we we know
that because again, the dollar bond
volatility, currency volatility, global
interest rates are all countercyclical
leading indicators of global liquidity.
When they break out to bullish rams, you
know liquidity in real time, even though
we can't track it in real time, is
breaking down uh to bearish rams. And
so, we'll see how long it lasts,
obviously, um you know, it's a function
of uh the geopolitical conflict. And uh
if I'm uh you know, again, I'm not
rooting for Iran, obviously, but if I'm

it's going to cost it's going to cost uh

uh in the right uh community. So, uh the

highlight our signal failure uh on gold
uh recently uh is because, you know, I
think there's a big difference in what
we're trying to do here uh for our
global investor community relative to
the folks who typically sit in my seat,
whose job it is, you know, they they
mostly focus on new subscribers, new
subscribers, new subscribers, more
revenue.
I could care less if we ever acquire
another new subscriber. We don't need
any new subscribers. What I need is for
our current subscribers to have the best
possible outcomes. And so, we can't do
that. Uh we you know, we're never going
to um maximize and optimize um you know,
our our process and our content for our
current subscribers to have the best
possible outcomes if we don't attack our
uh you know, uh mistakes with with
humility uh and attack it with the uh
insights and the in- intellect and and
more importantly, the academic rigor
that's required to fix it. So, just kind
of um you know, quickly highlighting
this on the signal failure on gold,
you know, one of the things that uh was
contributory to that uh that we uh
learned uh was the our volatility just
momentum signal for gold was programmed
like a currency. I mean, gold's been a
currency for almost, you know, for 6,000
plus years. But what we've learned in
the last kind of 18 months or so is that
gold's relationship between price and
volatility have actually evolved um you
know, materially evolved. And so, gold
is now behaving more like a traditional
commodity where there's sort of a supply
risk premium in the embedded into the
marketplace in terms of the relationship
between a price and volatility which you
know we
you know we you know did the due
diligence to understand that so that we
could reprogram the model so that the
model would be more responsive to draw
downs like this.
You know and then another thing that we
did
was you know amend our kiss system to to
apply the top down risk management
overlay back to gold. Recall that we
removed it heading into 2025 which
helped us maximize upside capture last
year. Recall that when we had the draw
down that we you know accurately
predicted fundamentally and also traded
really well from a kiss of death from a
macro perspective that gold really did
really well and performed you know
positively and and created positive
results in that draw down which is not
something it would have done had we had
that top down risk management overlay.
So you can make the case that we got
lucky on that last year and so
responding to this this most recent
episode of signal failure we're actually
adding back that top down risk
management overlay in gold to our
systematic kiss system in order to
optimize the model to prevent these
types of you know type two errors ever
again. And so you know the key takeaway
here is that you know
we're not running some you know
investment content gong show whereby the
whole goal is to tickle your amygdala
and and and and sell you bull porn if
you're the kind of person who likes to
munch on bull porn or sell you bear porn
if you're the kind of person who likes
to munch on bear porn. That's not what
we're doing here. This is an
institutional grade risk management
overlay. It's an institutional grade
macro risk management product. We have
many of the top hedge funds, top asset
managers, top pension funds, top
insurance funds, top family offices, top
wealth management platforms all here
seeing me our content and consuming our
content. I think we're up to the
somewhere close to 30 trillion dollars
in aggregate AUM across our client base
and so you know this is
you know I just want to you know bring
this up because again this is not
something you typically see from folks
who sit in the seat like I do. Most
people tend to hide from mistakes.
I've seen people I've worked with people
who actually lied through their teeth
about the mistakes and tell you that
they didn't make any mistakes. But I'll
be very honest that we we we did not get
our invest global investor community out
of gold as fast as we should have
last week
you know prior to last week because we
did get them out of stocks and we got
them out of credit. We got them out of a
lot of risk assets going back several
weeks ago and throughout the

So by and large our systems are working
pretty you know very well extremely well
probably much better than most systems
out there. However with this one
particular asset that is really core to
what we do here from a kiss perspective
in terms of gold our signals had
failure. So we had to acknowledge the
failure apologize for the failure. Part
of it was I was just traveling last week
so I didn't get to it as soon as I
probably should have. I should have.

for the failure and then ultimately

so that this particular you know we
don't have this types of type of mistake
again. So
the reason I bring all this up and bring
all this up publicly is because I want
you guys to recognize the difference
in what we're doing here at 42 macro in
terms of helping investors achieve their
strategic investment objectives versus
what a lot of folks who do what I do for
a living
you know relative to what they're doing
out there which is you know they're
focused on subscribe subscriptions
selling subscriptions subscriber
acquisition. And so you can't ever be
honest if you're always trying to
acquire new subscribers. You always got
to lie and tell everybody everything's
great. That's not my goal.
My goal is not to tell you everything's
great. My goal is to learn from mistakes
so that we can improve our systems
and optimize ourselves for having
achieved the success that we all are
used to achieving here at 42 macro. So
hopefully that was helpful. I'll go wrap
